How do stereotypes about girls' mathematical skills affect them early on in life and later on in their careers?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the text, the author discusses how girls must be brave to overcome stereotypes and participate in math. How is this an example of bravery? Describe a time when you or someone you know challenged a stereotype. How did it feel to do this?

Women don't pursue careers related to math as often as men do. Can you think of other careers that are largely occupied by men? Which careers tend to be filled by women? Why do you think these gender gaps exist?

In the text, the author discusses the importance of girls having role models to look up to in math-related careers. Why do you think it's important for girls to see women in math-related careers? How else do you think we could improve girls' involvement in math?

articles.title: Unexpected Journeys: An Introduction to Fantasy Literature
articles.author: Thomas Pool
articles.description: Thomas Pool discusses the characteristics of the fantasy genre and why it is popular.
articles.intro: The genre of fantasy has many super fans - from The Lord of the Rings to Harry Potter . In this article, Thomas Pool explains its popularity and defines the genre's characteristics.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, make note of the details that support why fantasy is such a popular genre.
articles.permissions: "Unexpected Journeys: An Introduction to Fantasy Literature" by Thomas Pool. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Far From Home

The young man was far from home. The sounds of battle rang out all around him. He gripped his weapon tight as he waded through the thick, slippery marsh. His helmet began to slide down his forehead, blocking his vision. He couldn’t stop. Duty urged him forward, though danger waited ahead.
The ground beneath his feet trembled from the sound of a deafening roar. He stumbled and tripped. As he struggled to his feet, he looked down and saw the ghostly faces of the fallen.

The year was 1916. Lieutenant Tolkien would never forget the sight of those faces in the watery mud of the Somme. Tolkien’s experiences during the First World War would go on to shape his grand saga, The Lord of the Rings .
Where Dreams Take Flight

Fantasy literature began in the late 19th century and found its stride during the mid 20th century. The genre of fantasy falls under the umbrella of speculative fiction , or stories that are not based in the real world. To “speculate” means to wonder about something or come up with ideas about something. There are many types of speculative fiction, like science fiction and superhero fiction. Sometimes, the lines between them can blur, but like fantasy, they have clear characteristics.

Fantasy is unique because of its magical and supernatural elements. Fantasy literature can mirror our world, but it is not necessarily tied to our laws of science and nature. Features of the genre include magical powers, dangerous or helpful creatures, and, of course, adventure. Fantasy has roots in folklore, myths, and fairy tales.

One of the most common elements in fantasy is the “Hero’s Journey.” The term was coined by literature professor Joseph Campbell. It describes the cycle of many fantasy stories. The journey is divided into the three acts. First is the Departure . This is when the hero must travel beyond their comfortable world. Next is the Initiation , when obstacles come between the hero and their goal. The hero must tackle their final challenge before they can move on to the end of the journey. In the Return , the hero goes back to the life they led before. Because of their journey, they have achieved freedom from fear.
Subgenres of Fantasy
Fantasy can be divided into three different subgenres. These are magical realism, low fantasy, and high fantasy.

Magical realism is set in the real world but with magic in it. Gabriel García Márquez’s classic One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967) is the best known work of magical realism. The magical elements are accepted and even unacknowledged by the characters. To them, magic is as normal as brushing your teeth.

Low fantasy is set in the real world too, but the hero is usually drawn into a separate, magical one. This happens in stories like The Wonderful Wizard of Oz (1900) and Alice in Wonderland (1865). This new world is unexpected, and the characters must find a way to return home.

High fantasy is set in a different world with its own laws, customs, and histories. Magic is typically built into the fabric of this world and has its own set of rules. High fantasy sometimes draws inspiration from the European Middle Ages, although this is not always true today. The most notable work of high fantasy is The Lord of the Rings (1954).
One Trilogy to Rule Them All

J.R.R. Tolkien’s trilogy The Lord of the Rings , along with its prequel The Hobbit (1937), became the gold-standard of fantasy literature. When people think of fantasy, they think of Tolkien. His work thrust fantasy into the mainstream during the 1900s. It has had a lasting effect on popular culture ever since.

Tolkien’s books are considered the height of fantasy due to their vivid world-building. He developed cultures, religions, and customs for his Middle Earth. He even invented new languages. This level of detail pulls the reader into the fictive dream . This refers to the almost dream-like state a reader falls into when reading a captivating story. In this state, the reader feels like they are a part of the imaginary world.

Tolkien’s service during WWI strongly informed his writing. His books feature heart-wrenching scenes of battle and loss based on his experiences. Because of this, they feel more emotional and, ironically, real. It also teaches the reader important lessons about war, friendship, and grief.
Magic and Mayhem

While Tolkien set a high bar in fantasy writing, other authors have certainly risen to meet it. One example is Ursula K. Le Guin. As a speculative fiction writer, she often straddled the divide between science fiction and fantasy. She is known for her groundbreaking 1968 Earthsea series.

Le Guin’s writing was decidedly feminist in a genre that was then controlled almost entirely by male authors. She wrote about female characters when most fantasy heroes were male. She also wrote about characters who were not white in a time when it was assumed that fantasy characters were only white.

Like Tolkien, she used her imagination to talk about important subjects and create a critical lens of the world through her writing. Her work is known for its cultural and historical criticism, particularly of the Vietnam War and colonialism.
Fantastic Stories and Where to Find Them

Fantasy is one of the most popular genres in media today. For example, the Harry Potter (1997) series has inspired millions of readers around the globe. It has enchanted even more on the silver screen.

Fantasy writers like Tolkien and Le Guin laid the groundwork for authors to capture the imaginations of readers today. If you are looking to read more fantasy literature, there are many new and established writers. Here are just a few: Rick Riordan, Rebecca Roanhorse, Nnedi Okorafor, Neil Gaiman, Tamora Pierce, Philip Pullman, Leigh Bardugo, Garth Nix, V.E. Schwab, N.K. Jemisin, Tomi Adeyami, and S.A. Chakraborty.

There are many things to love about fantasy. It offers a wide range of characters, plots, worlds, and themes — so there’s always something new to explore. It transports readers to new realms. It gives them hope and excitement.

Fantasy also helps us understand our world by pulling us into one that exists only in our imaginations. It makes us think about what shapes our world, why things happen, and what we can do to make it better. After all, we all need a little magic in our lives.

options.label: Fantasy can be similar to our world but contains magic and/or the supernatural.
Fantasy was at its best in the mid 1900s with J.R.R. Tolkein and Ursula K. LeGuin.
Fantasy is better when authors use their personal experiences for their stories.
Fantasy today is better when viewed on the big screen, instead of just read. E. Fantasy is popular because it helps readers understand their own world.

questions.prompt: What are TWO main ideas in the article?

options.label: They demonstrate how experiences can influence fantasy to make it feel real.
They explain why Tolkien set The Lord of the Rings during World War I.
They show how authors use their imagination to create new worlds.
They highlight that the real world can be scarier than fantasy.

questions.prompt: How do paragraphs 1-3 contribute to the development of ideas in the article?

options.label: "Fantasy literature began in the late 19th century and found its stride during the mid 20th century." (Paragraph 4)
"One of the most common elements in fantasy is the 'Hero's Journey.' The term was coined by literature professor Joseph Campbell." (Paragraph 6)
"Like Tolkien, she used her imagination to talk about important subjects and create a critical lens of the world through her writing." (Paragraph 16)
"There are many things to love about fantasy. It offers a wide range of characters, plots, worlds, and themes — so there's always something new to explore." (Paragraph 19)

questions.prompt: Which statement best supports the author's idea that fantasy "helps us understand our world by pulling us into one that only exists in our imagination" (Paragraph 20)?

options.label: If fantasy is written well, readers will not realize it is fiction.
Harry Potter is the most influential fantasy series written to date.
Fantasy is limited by the restrictions authors must place on their own imaginations.
Fantasy is popular because it helps readers understand the world while also providing hope.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the author's point of view in the article?
How does the author convey their point of view in the article? Use details from the text to support your response.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the article, Pool argues that fantasy helps us to understand our world. Do you agree with him? Why or why not? What helps you to better understand your world? How can you help others to understand the world?

Fantasy stories allow writers to use their imagination to think about the real world in a new, unique way. Think of a real world problem that you, a friend, or someone else you know had to overcome — or maybe a problem that a character in a book or movie had to overcome. How was the problem solved? Was the solution obvious to others? Did the hero have to think outside the box to solve it? How does using our imaginations help us to solve problems? In what way does fantasy help us understand the world?

articles.title: Old Games, New Territory
articles.author: Khat Patrong
articles.description: A young basketball player begins a new life in Florida after his father dies.
articles.intro: In this short story, a young basketball player begins a new life in Florida.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, make note of the details that support how the characters handle their emotions.
articles.permissions: "Old Games, New Territory" by Khat Patrong. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: You unlock the door to your aunt’s condo, and walk through the parking lot toward the basketball court with your ball in hand. Floridian palm trees shiver in the breeze and lizards scurry across your path. It feels like a travel show. Like life’s being played out on an Instagram post. Like you’re on vacation. Except you’re not.

There aren’t any palm trees where you’re from. There are basketball courts, yes, but they’re usually covered in snow this time of the year. This one is littered with people of all different backgrounds. Shirtless guys versus the shirts. You watch a tall topless, skinny guy pivot and fire off a mid-range jump shot. The ball swooshes the net without touching the rim. You picture your dad at the fence applauding.

You’re drenched already, and you ain’t even bounced a ball yet. Aunt Hilda told you it would be like this; that you would be trading snow pants for cargo shorts, sleds and shovels for slides and wife beaters. But you miss sledding season — the steep hills at the park on Dumbarton Street, where you and your friends would pat the snow down with your shovels until it was smooth; smooth enough to slide on until the snow melted away.

Toronto is behind you now, whether you like it or not. You look over towards the coastline as you walk between the parked cars. There are more trees, a broad walk and crashing waves. A moth floats by and high above two condors cruise in long sweeping circles. Yes, this could be a scene from an Instagram feed. But you’d give all the Canadian dollars you have left to the first person who could show you a social media post that captures the heat, the loneliness, the sticky-icky feeling you feel both inside and out. You don’t want to be here. There’s no way you do this on your own. Aunt Hilda pretty much threw you out of the condo. “Today, you’re going to make friends” she said, like that was a cinch. “This is your home now,” she said, like that was a good thing.

You stand at the edge of the court and dribble the ball, a kind of mini-warm up for you; a crossover, a between the legs move. Basketball’s a universal language. A girl whips by on a skateboard. The ball hits her knee and spins towards some hedges. She throws a faint “Sorry!” behind her as she pushes off towards the nearby park. One of the guys on the court spots you, and motions you over as you retrieve the ball. “Hey,” he says, “do you wanna play?” He has an accent, dark hair, thick eyebrows and tanned skin. He looks a little darker than you. You’re guessing he might be Dominican. He looks — they all look — around the same age as you. You nod.
“Name?” He says.
“Rashid.” You drop your ball onto the grass by the fencing.
“Carlos,” he replies and points across the court to where there’s some activity by the other exit. “Brian’s gotta go, we could use one more. Skins are kicking our butts.”

So you’re on the Shirts team. On the one hand, you’re thankful that you won’t have to expose your pooch to the world, but you’re so not overjoyed with the idea of going up against the skinny guy with the Kevin Durant-style jump shot. And with good reason. The game resumes and he starts hitting threes like you all are keeping score, hitting lay-ups whenever he feels like it, and collecting rebounds like they’re grocery store coupons. It’s like this for a little while. You shed a couple litres of sweat just chasing Durant’s shadow, and there’s not even a cloud in sight to grant you any mercy from the heat.

The game feels like a wrap; it’s so one sided. Your “Shirts” are taking a beating, and you might drop like this, wilting between the heat and, well... the heat that Durant and his shirtless team are bringing you. But the game changes when one of your guys goes down with what might be a cramp or a stitch or just plain old shame. That’s when Carlos calls for Gigi to get into the game.
Cue the uproar.
Aww nah, she’s a cheat.
Aww nah, she starts fights.
Come on, she’ll slow the game down!
Come on, she plays soft.

Even Durant gets in on the complaints. And you don’t need to have been in town long to understand where it’s all coming from. Fear is fear in any country. But Gigi looks harmless and none too bothered whether she plays or not. She stands on the sidelines, leaning against the fence. She’s tanned, like Carlos, with long, shoulder length ash-brown hair. She wears a white t-shirt with pink sleeves and blue athletic shorts. You don’t recognize her without a helmet, and but for the skateboard at her feet, you might not have recognized her at all. She giggles as the Shirts and the Skins come close to duking it out over whether or not she can play.

“Take Dwayne,” one of the boys on the Skins finally says. “We’ll take Gigi.” And then he turns to her like: “Gigi, you’re on our side. Take your top off for us.” He laughs, but no one else laughs along with him. She frowns. Nods. She doesn’t like this. It seems to make up her mind for her. She walks onto the court and joins the Shirts team. There’s a chorus of groans.

No one needs to tell you that she’s about to kill this game, but you wonder where all this fuss will come from. You wonder how she will kill this game. She is tall enough, but not extremely so, far from the tallest, inches shorter than the Skins’ Durant.

But then the game starts again; oh man, you can see where all the fuss lies. The girl doesn’t need height. She is the fastest on the court, shoots threes better than anyone else and has a mean mid-range. The Skins resort to fouls; they nudge and they elbow; they body her off the ball. If you had an official, they’d be calling fouls and she’d be taking (and making) free throws all day. But, instead, whenever she falls (or is otherwise thrown) to the asphalt and scuffs her knee or grazes an elbow, she just shakes her head, curses, gets up and keeps it moving. You like that. You think you’re in love. But you know you’re not.

Truth be told, you realize as the game goes on that she is fighting back; she is just doing so on the court. Saving it for when she has the ball, which is often because the team tactic by now is to get the rock to her whenever she is open. She steps up her moves with each foul that goes uncalled — spin moves, jukes, no-look passing. There’s no doubt in your mind that this sister must play for her school, that scouts must hog the sidelines at that school, and that someday she is going to play at the college level and beyond. She throws a sidestep move that almost breaks Durant’s ankles, that leaves him on the floor and the onlookers in laughter. That’s something else you notice, the crowd that’s gathered, that’s grown since Gigi came in, like they all knew beforehand that she was going to give them a show.

This Durant character, who by now you’ve heard them call Justin, gets up and turns into a monster. Cussing and everything — just damn ugly. And the Skins take their lead from him, throwing abuse alongside elbows now. They call her “Dora,” “Pink Curry” (after Steph Curry, I know, not very clever), and “The Broad Baller,” and you can tell from the way she responds with just “And still better than you, and still better than you,” that she has been in this position before and has been taking this abuse for a long time now.

“And still better than you,” she says, again, as you pass her the ball. You don’t even know what Justin has called her this time around. He takes a defensive position up in front of her. She starts dribbling, passing the ball between her legs a couple of times, then she fakes a shot and Justin goes sprawling back, ankles broken again. There’s more uproar from the sidelines, and Justin just can’t live in the embarrassment and laughter again, so as Gigi jumps to actually shoot, he outstretches a long leg, and clips her feet from under her, sending her crashing to the ground. This time it’s a hard fall, chin first.

You, like other members of the Shirts team, run to her aid. But this isn’t enough. You don’t know why. Something’s rustling under your skin. Everything’s got in you: The fouls have risen to the level of dirty play. And this latest foul wasn’t a foul at all, just straight violence, which couldn’t be anything further from the idea of sports, or even gamesmanship. The constant abuse between the two teams, the smack talk between friends that doesn’t sound like how you should talk to friends. The fact that some of it has been directed towards you, towards the weight you are carrying. Then there’s the crush you've been steadily developing for Gigi. The blood on her knee. It’s all in you. Is this what home looks like now?

You think back to playing ball on the court with your dad, the way he would run you through drills every day after school. “Hey kiddo,” he’d say. “Remember to think one step ahead of your opponent.” You’re pretty sure your dad didn’t mean it like this, but you pick up the ball anyway and throw it, and you make sure it hits Justin square in the face before you rush at him screaming.

Oh man, playing hero — bad idea . Both Skins and Shirts turn against you. They pull you off him, they punch you, they stomp you out as you curl up into a ball. They kick your butt, some. What were you thinking? Nah, they’re not condoning Justin’s behavior. But this is more of a case of defending one their own. I mean, who are you to come at one of their friends swinging punches and taking names? You’re an outsider invited in to play with them. They use their fists to remind you of this.

Fortunately, most of them tire quickly in the sun, the others are pulled off of you. Light bird chatter fills the air. You listen as everyone catches their breath. All you can do is just focus on the pain, which is everywhere, the back of your head, the ribs, the knees. You think your nose might be broken. It’s bleeding heavily. “Make friends,” your aunt said. Boy, you’re trying your best.

Gigi kneels beside you and helps you sit up. You can see how spent everyone is. How they’ve gathered in the shade to cool off. The game is over and, seeing this, another group of boys on the sidelines takes over the court. Gigi says something, but you can’t make it out. Your ears are ringing. She puts her hands on your shoulders and helps you to your feet. You touch your nose. Blood comes away on your fingertips.
On the sidelines, in the shade, Gigi reaches into her neon pink fanny pack and takes out a tissue. “Here, this might help stop the blood,” she says. “It’s Rashid,” right?
You nod. “Gigi.”
“Nah,” she says. “It’s Gisele to you.”
“Are you okay?” you ask. Her chin is grazed.
“Me?” she says, and chuckles. She waves the tissue. You take it and apply pressure to your nose. You wince with the pain.
You reel off a string of curses, threats, things you’re going to do but not really going to do to Justin. “They’re out of line,” you say, but then stop when you see Gisele smiling. “What?”
“You didn’t have to jump him like that.”
“Huh?” you reply. You don’t understand what she means by this. “He kicked you while you were shooting!”
“Boy,” she replied. “You ain’t my savior; don’t be acting like you saving me. I know how to deal with these boys. I’ve been dealing with them for years.”

Gisele picks up her skateboard. “I’ve known those buttheads since middle school. And that one kid since elementary. They weren’t always like this,” she says. “But nowadays things are hard, you know? It’s like, for many of them, all they have is basketball and then I come along and take it away from them, you know?”
“What do you mean by ‘take it away?’”
“Like, I might be that reminder, you know?”
“Of what?”
“Dude,” she says, “where are you from?”
“Hey, Gigi,” one of the new boys on the court calls out, “come on in.”
She laughs, and shakes her head. “Nah, I’ve smacked you guys around enough for one day.”
“I’m from Canada,” you say. “I just moved here.”

You watch her eyes light up. Her whole attitude changes. “Canada?” she says. “Cool.” She opens her fanny pack again and removes two bubblegum lollipops, as if to reward you for the knowledge, but then again, the flavor she hands you is Sour Apple. “My parents took me to Vancouver once, is that where you’re from?” She unwraps her lollipop. You remove the tissue from your nose, and tell her that you’re from Toronto. Gisele hands you another tissue. Your nose is still doing its best impression of Niagara Falls. “We better go to my house,” she says. “My grandma will be able to tell if it’s broken or not.” She tucks her skateboard under her arm. Her helmet hangs from the crook of her other elbow. You stuff the new tissue into your nostrils and try your best to not give away any visible signs of discomfort. You retrieve your ball and toss the old blood-soaked tissue in the trash as you make your way towards the condo complex. “I’ve never been to Vancouver,” you say, your voice sounding too nasal for your liking.

Gisele smiles. “That’s where my parents were stationed when I was a baby. We were there for a year before we moved back to the States.” You think you can detect a little sadness in her voice. The two of you walk past your building and towards building 4, where you make your way up the steps to the second floor. You pass a neighbor struggling with trash bags in each hand.

You arrive at her door and make a note of the number: 11, for future reference. She struggles with the key in the lock, but then the door opens and a slightly older and taller version of Gisele is standing in the doorway. The older girl wears a Naruto t-shirt and stonewashed jeans. She crosses her arms, looks at you and the ball you’re holding, frowns and says, “You just don’t listen, Gigi. You keep playing basketball with those boys and you’re going to get injured. Your coach has told you this.” She lifts Gisele’s chin. “This time your face, what if next time it’s your knee or your ankle?” She looks to the heavens. And then at you again. “Is this the nose your chin made friends with?”

Gisele pushes past her sister. “Abuelita!” she cries as she disappears down the hall. You stand in the doorway, her older sister inspecting the mess out of you. It feels like you’re in trouble. Her sister turns but then looks back at you. “Well, come in, come in. We don’t want any more mosquitos inside.” You shut the door, take off your shoes and place them neatly beside your ball at the front door. “What in the world?” Gisele’s sister says, then calls out down the hall. “Yo, Gigi, where’d you find this one from? A boy, taking off his shoes without being asked. What next?” She walks into the kitchen area. You take in the colorful hibiscus wallpaper, the smell of warm spice that is somehow both savory and sweet.
“Rasheeeed!” you hear Gigi call from down the hallway. The way she says your name... like it begins with an “L.”

Jesus is on the wall in the living room, next to some family portraits — Gisele and her older sister are in most of them. All sorts of trinkets line the mantel and there are plants in every corner. Sunlight spills through the window blinds. Her grandma is sitting at a table against the wall, despite the sofas looking a lot more comfortable. She has grey hair, held in big curls. Her skin is tanned and leathered. She wears a cardigan over her dress and looking at her feels like you’re back in the north, back in the comfort of the cold. She gestures for you to come closer, “Ven aquí, niño.”

As soon as you’re within grasp, Gisele’s grandma grabs you and squeezes your nose bridge. Oh man, you want to flip, but your dad taught you better. Gisele’s grandma looks at Gisele as if to say: Why are you wasting my time? “It’s no broken,” she says. “Keep the tissue there.”

You don’t know if it’s the pain, or exhaustion, the heat or even all three, but as you nod your head you begin to feel light-headed. Your eyes roll back, and just like that your knees buckle and give out from underneath you.

You open your eyes to find Jesus is staring at you. Sunlight beams gently through the window. The smell of onions and peppers wafts up to your nose. You remove the wet hand towel from your forehead and sit up. You’re lying on the sofa. There’s a fresh bandage over the bridge of your nose.
“Hello?” you cry, and you can hear the excitement gather from the kitchen.
“Ah,” the voice of Gisele’s grandma cries out. “You are awake from sleepy time!”

Moments later, she walks into the room holding a tray of juice and a sandwich. “Too much heat,” she says, as she lays down the tray next to you. The sandwich is steaming hot and it smells so good you start to salivate. Your cell phone is next to the tray. You pick it up, expecting to find several missed calls from Aunt Hilda. Gisele’s grandma is watching you, expecting you to expect this. “We called Hilda, told her you were fine,” she says.
“You know my aunt?”

She chuckles. “Sí, Gisele told me you had a really bad skateboarding accident. You hit your head that hard, eh?” She moves to the table, and sits down in the same spot as before, and when she looks at you again she realizes that you still haven’t put it all together.

“Me,” she says, gesturing to herself. “I am the one who your aunt called. I told her I will send for Gigi to take you to the skateboard park. I suppose it is my fault you hurt yourself. I told your aunt this. Apologized. I’ve known your aunt for a very long time. She’s such a sweet woman. Helps me take care of my plants.” She gestures to all the foliage in the room with a sweep of the arm. There’s a lot.

You pick up the juice and clean it with one gulp. “You’ll get used to the heat,” she says, but by now you have a bite of the sandwich in your mouth and can only grunt a response. Gisele’s grandma chuckles, plucks a magazine off the table, opens it and rustles the pages. “I sent Gigi and Dani to the store. They should be back soon,” she says, glancing at the window. You eat in silence, listening to the clock tick away, the radio playing soft Spanish music and the faint sound of kids playing outside.

You don’t know what to do once the sandwich is done. Small talk has never been your forte. You always wind up saying the wrong thing. You fiddle with your thumbs. You’re not too sure if you should head back home or if you should stay and wait for Gisele to return.
“Was I knocked out?” you ask.
“Hmmm,” Gisele’s grandma replies. “You fainted. Too much heat.”
“For long?”
Gisele’s grandma doesn’t look away from the magazine. “Long enough to make a sandwich.”
“Do I need a doctor?”
“We all need doctors.”
“Where are Gisele’s parents?” you ask. And there it is. The wrong thing. You can’t help yourself.

Gisele’s grandmother looks up at you. “They are no longer with us,” she says. But something about the way she says it makes you look away, the way she lays down the magazine, and adds, “There was a car accident, my dear. Both the girls were very young.” Your eyes land on the family portraits again, only this time you look harder. You find her parents among the portraits; in one picture they are in their military uniforms, another photo captures them during their wedding, a third photo with their children, Gisele still a small baby between them. You think of your father. His sudden sickness: prostate cancer. It all happened so quickly. Tears begin to well up in your eyes. You fake a cough and try to quickly wipe them away with your sleeve. It doesn’t work.
“Your father,” Gisele’s grandmother says. “I’m sorry, I should not talk like this.”
“You know?”
“Yes, Hilda mentioned having to travel to Canada... for her brother... I’m sorry.”

It is too late though, you are already thinking about Canada, about Toronto, about the lonely days that led up to the funeral. And then the funeral itself. You wore a pressed suit that was far too big and the baby blue with white polka dots tie that was your dad’s favorite. Your mother dressed in tattered black clothing, and explained that it was all they had at the shelter. Her hair piled high in a nest that hadn’t been combed, washed or even touched. You remember the grief, but you also remember feeling so embarrassed standing next to her. After seeing your dad, lying there motionless, all you wanted to hear was one of his jokes. You wanted to laugh. Instead, you remained silent through it all. Silent as those in attendance who prayed. Silent as those who lowered him into the earth.

“My dad used to train me in basketball,” you say aloud. “We used to go out to the park every day and train. If it was winter we’d go to the gym.” Gisele’s grandmother is the only other person in the room, in the condo, but you’re not really talking to her. You’re not really talking to anyone... at least, in your mind, not anyone who can hear you. “I used to get so tired of the same drills... over and over again. Same time, every day.” Gisele’s grandmother shifts in her seat but keeps her gaze soft as she listens. “And now, I’d give anything to play with my dad again. All those moments are just... gone.” You wipe your eyes, but it is too late for any of that now. You are sobbing up a storm at this point. You just don’t know what to do with yourself.

Gisele’s grandmother gets up and walks over to the cabinet. She opens a drawer and is shuffling a few items around before she turns and faces you again. “Yes, those moments are ‘gone,’” she says, “but that doesn’t mean your father isn’t still with you.” She walks over to you and places a pebble in your palm, a blueish-grayish looking thing. “Moonstone,” she says. “It helps us deal with healing. It helps us deal with change. Your father is with you because you are right here. Right where you belong.” You turn the stone over in your hands. It feels cold and smooth; it has some weight to it, too. “You’re going to be alright, dear. Cry, scream, laugh, but don’t forget to love.” She places a hand on your shoulder and drops a box of tissues into your lap. She doesn’t say anything. All you can hear is the rough breaks of silence as you gasp for air. She leaves the room and you know it’s to give you some space, some time alone.

Gisele’s grandmother does not return until Gisele and Daniela are back from the store, their bags rustling with ingredients for tonight’s feast. By then, you’re thankful that you’ve managed to get yourself together. Gisele’s grandmother invites you to stay for dinner. Gisele sets up her PlayStation while you wait for the food to be prepared. You show her that Canadians can crush Fortnite , too. When Gisele grows bored of losing, you switch to watching some WNBA, and she walks you through almost every move Diana Taurasi makes, some before she even makes them. Daniela rolls her eyes and accuses her of showing off.

Dinner is served. Rice, corn, refried beans and crispy fried chicken. You eat well, and you talk around the table like you’ve known the family for years. You only wish Aunt Hilda was there with you all. You discover that you and Daniela share a love for Animal Crossing , and the sisters talk about the neighborhood. Where to go (the ice cream shack down the block), where to avoid (the CVS on Barton, because past that, was the bad side of town). They talk about the local high school, how it’s big but not so bad. Homeroom is by last name, and since yours is Harris, it is likely that you’re going to end up in the same room as Gisele. Between all this, the jokes and the laughter, you begin to feel a little lighter and even forget all about your nose until you take a trip to the bathroom and catch your reflection in the mirror.

Gisele offers to walk you the short distance home. “In case Justin is still out there,” she says, but you both know that ain’t it. You leave the condo with a chest full of gratitude, your basketball tucked under one arm and a bag full of tupperware in the other. There’s enough food to see you and your aunt through the weekend.

It’s quiet as you make your way down the steps and set out towards the building, and, as previously mentioned, you don’t know what to do with silence, so again you just can’t help yourself. “Your grandma told me about your parents,” you say.
“Yeah,” she replies. “Do you know what the worst thing is? I don’t remember much about them. I try my best to hold on to my memories and it scares me that they might be fading.”
“Wow, do you know what my problem is?”
“I can guess,” Gisele replies.
“I remember too much, and I’m worried that it will always be like this.”
“It won’t,” she says. “I mean, I’m pretty sure it won’t be. It’s still... new.”

“I know,” you reply as you move between the cars in the parking lot. By now the sun has long gone down but it is still hot and humid, and though you have observed this from the windows of your aunt’s condo over the last few days, it still feels crazy to be out at night in this kind of heat. You stop nearby the basketball court, near the bushes where hours before you retrieved your basketball. “Hey, Gigi,” you hear a voice call out, and realize that there are one or two people still out on the court, even though it is dark. “You want to play Horse?”
“Nah, I gotta get home,” Gisele cries out.
“You told your grandma that I hurt myself while skateboarding...” you say, once she turns back to you.
“Oh, yeah. She would’ve freaked out if she’d found out I got these cuts and bruises while playing basketball.”
“Because they’re bullies, Gisele.”

She laughs, “That’s not why. Coach, Dani, Grandma — they’re all so protective of me now. They don’t want me playing basketball in the park anymore. They don’t want me risking injury and messing up my chances of going to college.”
“Well,” you reply, “I can see why they would be worried.” You nod towards the park. “They’re playing bully ball out there, not — ”
“You don’t understand...”
“I know, I know. It’s like everyone wants to play with you...”

“Yeah,” she replies. “I mean, I get that. That’s true. I’m good and people want to play with me. I know that, and I don’t say it to show off. I’m lucky. I’ve been blessed, but I work hard at my game, too.”
“That’s even more reason why you shouldn’t let them call you names.”

“It ain’t cool, and they are going to have to learn that. I play with them because those are my people. I got to show them love. They know I’ve got a good chance of getting a scholarship and that makes them happy, but that also makes them mad. Like you shouldn’t have to be a good free-throw shooter to get a chance at life, but it can feel like that sometimes, have a spectacular talent or bust. Sometimes they be gettin’ on my nerves. I have a ticket out, at least that’s what they think, but even if I make it to college, my parents are not going to be there to see me graduate, and I just want to tell them: shut up, you get to go home to a family, your mami and papi. You’ve got everything. Shut up.” She sucks her teeth.
You both start to walk again, in the direction of your aunt’s place, which you feel won’t be so hard to call home after all, and for a moment it is quiet but for the sound of the nearby waves.

“You know,” Gisele says, “when my grandma told me to come out and meet you, to catch you before you went too far, I was going to grab you, tell you who I was, and take you to the park. But then I see you out here with a basketball, trying to perfect a crossover, failing at it, and I’m like: the skate park, for what? The hipsters and the skaterheads? The best and worst parts of the neighborhood are all in one place, on that court. Let me see what this cat is made of...”
“Wait,” you say, as the two of you stop outside your aunt’s ground floor condo. “Your grandma sent you out this evening to come and find me; to hang out with me?”
“Yep,” Gisele says and nods her head, “I guess she and your aunt felt like I could understand what you were feeling.”
“You do?”

Gisele shakes her head. “Nah, I don’t think so.” But when your face falls flat, she adds, “I was still a baby when it all happened.” Still, it’s not enough to lighten the sudden heaviness in the air and, as if she knows this, she asks, “Did she give you a stone?” and when you nod, her eyes light up. “I knew it, I knew it!” She punches the ball out from beneath your arm, chases it a little distance in the direction towards the courts, and when she retrieves it, she starts bouncing it between her legs. “Dani owes me money. I told her Nana would give you a stone.”
“You have one?”
“Uh-huh,” she says, “my sister, too. Cry, scream, laugh — ”
“But don’t forget to love.” You both say at the same time and that’s more than a little awkward. She starts mimicking your warm-up routine with the ball.
You smile. “Did you run into me on purpose?”

Gisele laughs. “Well, technically, I ran into your ball. I wanted to make sure I had the right person. And when I saw that I did, I figured: why take you to the skatepark? This here,” she gestures to the basketball court, “is your home now and those cats on the court are going to be your family, for better and for worse. And trust me, beyond the name calling, when push comes to shove, they’re going to have your back, just like they all had Justin’s back.”
“You don’t have to tell me,” you say. You watch her move from mimicking your routine with the ball to performing her own tricks. “Gisele...”
“Gigi.”
“What?”
She stops bouncing the ball. “Gigi,” she repeats. “You can call me Gigi.”
“Can I ask you something?”
“Sure.”
“Do you think you can coach me up a little some day?”
“Show you a few tricks?”
You nod. “Yeah.”
She throws a fake pump at you. “Sure, I think I can manage that. We’ll have you shooting in no time.”
“I can shoot,” you protest.
“Right...” she says. “I didn’t see you hit a single one.”
You both start laughing.
“Well, goodnight then,” you say.
“Yep,” she says and stops bouncing the ball. “This was fun, let’s hang again tomorrow.”

“That would be cool,” you say. You close your eyes and lean forward, lips puckered and feel them as they smack against... what you realize is your basketball. You open your eyes. Gisele is holding up in front of your face.
“Boy,” she says, “this is what you better learn to love.”
She pushes the ball into your stomach and you take it from her, she laughs again and shakes her head “See? You fitting in already.”
She turns and starts to walk away, shaking her head and smiling.
You call out behind her, “Same time, same basketball court. Tomorrow?”
She looks back and smiles but doesn’t stop walking. “Sounds good,” she says.

articles.title: Nature Shows How Dragons Might Breathe Fire
articles.subtitle: Reliably bringing all of the ingredients together without harming the dragon could, however, get explosive
articles.author: Bethany Brookshire
articles.description: Bethany Brookshire uses science to discuss how a dragon might breathe fire.
articles.intro: Fire-breathing dragons are a shared creature among many fantasy worlds. But how realistic is it that a dragon could breathe fire? In this informational text, Bethany Brookshire explores how a dragon might actually breathe fire.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the special characteristics of the real animals that the author references.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , 2018. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs or textbooks, visit: h ttps://societyforscience.org/permission-republish

blocks.text: No fantasy world is complete without a fire-breathing dragon. But if dragons were real, how might they get that fiery breath? Nature, it seems, has all the parts a dragon needs to set the world on fire. The creatures just require a few chemicals, some microbes — and maybe tips from a tiny desert fish.

Fire has three basic needs: something to ignite the blaze, fuel to keep it burning and oxygen, which interacts with the fuel as it burns. That last ingredient is the easiest to find. Oxygen makes up 21 percent of Earth’s atmosphere. The bigger challenges are sparking and fueling the flame.

All it takes to strike a spark is flint and steel, notes Frank van Breukelen. He’s a biologist at the University of Nevada, Las Vegas. If a dragon had an organ like a bird’s gizzard , it could store swallowed rocks. In birds, those rocks help break down tough foods. Swallowed flint might rub against some steel inside the dragon, sparking a flame. “Maybe what you have is sort of scales that are flint-like and click together,” van Breukelen says. If the spark was close enough to a very sensitive fuel, that might be enough to ignite it.

But some chemicals don’t need that initial spark. Pyrophoric molecules burst into flame the instant they contact air. Consider the element iridium , says Raychelle Burks. She is a chemist in Texas at St. Edwards University in Austin. Iridium burns different colors when it becomes part of various molecules. One of them burns a warm orange or red. Another burns a violet-blue. (That’s one way to get the blue flame of the zombie ice dragon in George R.R. Martin’s Game of Thrones series.)
Unfortunately, iridium isn’t common, especially in biology. “There are a lot of cool elements on the periodic table, but [living things] only use a few,” Burks explains.

There are other pyrophoric chemicals that a dragon might find a little closer to home, notes Matthew Hartings. He’s a chemist at American University in Washington, D.C. Assume that dragons like caves, he begins. “If you’re living amongst a bunch of rocks, you’ll have access to a high amount of iron.”

Iron can react with another chemical, hydrogen sulfide . This is a flammable gas that smells like rotten eggs. It is found in crude oil. When hydrogen sulfide and iron get together — in a rusty oil pipe, for example — the result is iron sulfide. Combine it with air and you’ve got an explosive mix. Iron sulfide is sometimes the culprit when gas pipelines or tanks blow up.

Another explosive option comes from Anne McCaffrey’s series The Dragonriders of Pern . McCaffrey describes her dragons chewing on rocks containing phosphine — a chemical made of one phosphorus atom and three hydrogen atoms. In gas form, phosphine is very flammable and explodes on contact with oxygen. It’s also very toxic: Just seven drops of its liquid form can kill someone.
Burning burps

Fictional dragons often spout flaming gas. But a gas would present problems, Hartings says. Gas, he notes, expands to fill available space. To keep it contained, a dragon would have to keep that gas under pressure.

Chemicals like phosphine, therefore, aren’t the perfect dragon-fire solution, Hartings says. The boiling point for phosphine is -84° Celsius (-120° Fahrenheit). At room (or dragon breath) temperature, it’s a gas. “You’d have to really compress it,” he says, to make it a liquid that a dragon could store and use.

Also, Hartings notes, gases are difficult to control. If a dragon blew some fiery gas into the wind, the flames might wash back on the creature and singe its face. “You have a much better chance of controlling your flame spray if you’re pushing liquid rather than a gas,” he explains.

A liquid also would help a dragon avoid burning itself, Hartings notes. The liquid with its flammable gas would ignite as soon as it hit air. Speed is key. “As long as you are shooting it out fast enough, [the] particles don’t hit the air until they are far enough away from your face,” he notes.

A combination of liquid and gas might work even better, Burks suggests. In an aerosol spray , tiny liquid droplets are suspended in a pressurized gas, which spurts out when it is released. If a dragon were to shoot an aerosol spray, it could look like a gas, with some of the properties of a liquid. “In a fine aerosol spray, it would look like the dragon is spraying fire,” Burks notes. The aerosol would spread out, she says, “and the minute it hits air — kaboom!”
Something fiery, something fishy
Plenty of liquids in nature will burn. Living things already produce two of these that might work for a dragon: ethanol and methanol . Both are alcohols often burned as fuels.

“Certainly, we know that yeast makes ethanol,” Hartings says. These single-celled fungi transform sugars into alcohol. That’s why they’re used to brew beer and make other alcoholic beverages. A dragon with a bellyful of yeast is not as silly as it might appear. Yeast are part of the microbial community that lives on and in people and other animals.

Methanol first requires methane. Ruminants — including cows, goats, giraffes and deer — make methane during digestion. Certain bacteria can turn methane into methanol, Hartings notes. A dragon that got enough fiber in its diet to make methane could pass that gas on to its bacterial buddies, which would convert it into methanol.

But those bacterial coworkers might not even be needed. The Devil’s Hole pupfish doesn’t bother with them. It is a tiny, incredibly rare species found in Devil’s Hole — a single naturally heated pool in Nevada. This fish can whip up its own whisky in a pinch, van Breukelen and his colleagues have shown.

Temperatures in Devil’s Hole reach 33 °C (91 °F). There is very little oxygen in the water to start with. When it gets hot, the oxygen levels drop even lower — too low for the fish to breathe. So pupfish stop using oxygen. Instead, they produce energy anaerobically — without oxygen. In the process, their bodies make ethanol.
The fish produce 7.3 times more ethanol than fish living in cooler water, notes van Bruekelen. He and his colleagues published their fishy findings in 2015 in the Journal of Experimental Biology .

A dragon might be able to produce ethanol under similar circumstances. However, van Breukelen says, it’s not quite so simple. “I don’t think there’s a way to keep ethanol. I don’t think you could store it,” he says. The reason: It seeps through everything. Ethanol, he explains “goes right through membranes .” Those include the membranes that surround cells and organs. When pupfish produce ethanol, the chemical ends up throughout the fish. It would not pool as a concentrate in some pouch or organ. So any dragon that made ethanol would have trouble storing enough to get a decent flame going.

The pupfish won’t be setting the world on fire — nor will dragons. One is a tiny fish, and the other isn’t real. Both, however, offer an excuse to use our imaginations to apply science to the fantastic.

options.label: By using science and their knowledge of real organisms, scientists can speculate about what would be necessary for a dragon to breathe fire.
Many fantasy authors use elements of science and nature to create their worlds and creatures such as fire-breathing dragons.
Science can help prove that even our wildest fantasies are possible, such as dragons' ability to breathe fire.
Creating believable fantasy creatures, such as fire-breathing dragons, requires creativity as well as a thorough understanding of science.

articles.title: The Quake That Shook Up Geology
articles.subtitle: A huge earthquake in Alaska 50 years ago triggered a shift in what geologists know about Earth
articles.author: Beth Geiger
articles.description: Beth Geiger discusses the Great Alaskan Earthquake of 1964.
articles.intro: On March 27, 1964, the second most powerful earthquake recoded in world history struck south-central Alaska. In this informational text, Beth Geiger discusses the effects of the earthquake felt around the nation and what scientists were able to learn from this event.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what happens with the Earth’s plates during earthquakes.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , 2018. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs or textbooks, visit: https://societyforscience.org/permission-republish

blocks.text: March 27, 1964, was a quiet day in Alaska. Yet as dusk approached on this Good Friday, just two days before Easter, a major upheaval was in store. At 5:36 p.m., the ground began shaking violently. Alaskans are familiar with earthquakes. Indeed, the state averages some 60 each day — about 22,000 a year. But this was no ordinary tremor. This was the Big One.
In Anchorage, roads buckled and sank. Houses pulled apart. Railroad tracks warped. In Valdez, the ground beneath the harbor slumped — and nearly swallowed a ship docked there.

Most earthquakes rattle the ground for just seconds. Maybe even for a half or full minute. But 50 years ago, that now infamous Alaska quake shook steadily and terrifyingly for almost five solid minutes.
“It was so large that it caused the entire Earth to ring like a bell,” observes seismologist Tom Brocher and his colleagues from the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) in a recent publication.
Then, as Alaska’s shaking ceased, things got worse — much worse.

The earthquake had uplifted large sections of seafloor. That movement generated huge and fast-moving waves in the open ocean, called tsunamis. In bays and inlets, the quake also triggered underwater landslides. These unleashed more tsunamis.

Together, these destructive waves swept Alaska’s coastline with devastating speed and power. In places, the waves towered as high as a 20-story building. They washed away everything in their immediate path — buildings, docks and people.

No Alaskan who survived would ever forget this day. By midnight, 131 people were dead. Most had been killed by the tsunami waves that raked not just the coast of Alaska, but Oregon and California too. Indeed, like ripples in a pond, a tsunami travels outward in all directions.

Quake effects were felt in nearly every state. In Washington, Seattle’s Space Needle swayed like a tree in the wind. Lakes sloshed back and forth in Texas. Similar sloshing swamped fishing boats in Louisiana.
The Great Alaskan Earthquake was a magnitude-9.2 event. The only stronger trembling ever recorded was a magnitude-9.5 monster that shook Chile in May 1960.

Now, a full half-century after Alaska’s gigantic quake, geologists are still feeling its effects. The reason: This quake opened a new era in geology. And it has forever changed what scientists understand about the constant remodeling of Earth’s surface.
A new worldview

In 1964, plate tectonics still was just an emerging — if unproven — concept. It held that Earth’s uppermost layer, called the lithosphere, is broken into enormous pieces. Each giant slab was expected to be moving slowly.
Now, 50 years later, plate tectonics is no longer a clever hypothesis. It’s a well-documented certainty.

Like a jigsaw puzzle, Earth’s lithosphere is divided into interlocking pieces, called plates. (The lithosphere is Earth’s outer solid layer. It includes the crust and uppermost mantle.) Depending on how you count them, there are about 12 main tectonic plates, and numerous smaller ones. Their thickness ranges from 80 to 200 kilometers (50 to 124 miles). Plates beneath the oceans are much thinner than those under continents. These tectonic plates move gradually, bumping and grinding against each other.

As Alaska’s state seismologist, Mike West studies earthquakes. It was only after the 1964 quake, he says, that scientists could definitively point to the effects of plate movements on Earth’s surface. “Before the 1964 quake,” he recalls, “you could look at features and say ‘Yes, these could be explained by plate tectonics. But they could also be explained by other things.’” Back then, he points out: “There was no smoking gun yet.”

Scientists now classify the bumping and grinding between plates in three different ways. Along convergent boundaries, neighboring plates either collide head-on or a denser ocean plate dives beneath a lighter continental plate. Along other, divergent boundaries, plates move away from each other. At still others, known as transform boundaries, plates slide past each other. The friction between these massive plates means they don’t move fast. Some start and then stop, only to start again much later.
Earth definitely moved with a start during the 1964 quake. But it took some geological sleuthing to determine how and why.
Finding proof

Geologist George Plafker worked for the USGS near San Francisco, Calif. He and two of his colleagues flew up, landing at an Air Force base outside Anchorage, Alaska, just a day after the main shaking stopped. Their mission: Figure out what had just happened.

They knew earthquakes develop when shifts occur along underground fractures, called faults. They still had many questions to answer. One was figuring out why some parts of Alaska had risen as much as 11.5 meters (38 feet), while the ground at other sites had dropped, or subsided . They also sought to locate the quake’s epicenter. The epicenter is that X-marks-the-spot site where the tremors commenced.

Plafker’s team mapped all the changes triggered by the quake that they could find. They were scouting for patterns. In particular, “We were looking for evidence of surface faulting,” he recalled at a press briefing earlier this week. Lucky for this team, 70 new seismographs (SIZE moh grafs) had recently been installed all over the world. These instruments detect and record earthquakes. By recording the size, direction and arrival times of the different seismic waves from a quake, seismographs help pinpoint its size and epicenter.

“It was the first global seismic network,” West tells Science News for Students . “So this was the first earthquake to be recorded around the world.” In the days and weeks that followed, dozens of smaller earthquakes continued to rattle Alaska. The seismographs recorded these aftershocks as well. Those instruments also helped identify the shape of the fault responsible for all the shaking.

Plafker studied all of these data. Eventually he arrived at a very important conclusion. The fault was not vertical. It wasn’t a break cutting straight down through deep rock, as many geologists had assumed. Like a descending ramp, the fault instead sloped at an angle, deep beneath Alaska.

Scientists next had to fit this finding to their understanding of plate tectonics. It turned out that the ramp’s shape was very, very significant. Plafker had confirmed that the earthquake occurred in a subduction zone . That’s a type of convergent plate boundary where one plate dives beneath another.
Alaska’s long southern coastline marks where the Pacific Plate, moving north, dives beneath the North American Plate. And as the Pacific Plate dives, it slopes downward.

“George’s interpretation of this as a subduction zone was a real key,” says Gary Fuis. He’s a geophysicist with the USGS who recently co-authored a study looking back at the 1964 earthquake. Explains Fuis: “This was some of the first clear evidence that plate tectonics was right.”
Sticky situation

The dynamics of a subduction zone explained why the 1964 earthquake was so massive. Tectonic plates constantly push against each other. They tend to subtly creep along. When a fault actually slips, the motion is both sudden and exaggerated. It’s like opening a sticky window or door. You push and push. Then suddenly, it pops open.

On a gigantic scale, that’s what happened in Alaska. The Pacific Plate had been creeping north about 6 centimeters (2.5 inches) per year. Then, all at once, the Pacific Plate thrust forward. It slipped as much as 18 meters (60 feet) under the North American plate, the geologists learned.
That motion was “huge,” West says. East to west, nearly 960 kilometers (600 miles) of fault had ruptured at once.

Plafker named this event a megathrust earthquake. These occur directly along subduction zones. And they are powerful. (For example, the 2011 magnitude-9.0 earthquake off the eastern coast of Japan that unleashed a huge tsunami also was a megathrust earthquake.) In fact, megathrust events are the largest type of earthquake on the planet, notes Peter Haeussler. He’s a research geologist with USGS and its Alaska coordinator of earthquake hazards research.
Natasha Ruppert at the Alaska Earthquake Center in Fairbanks is another seismologist. She now estimates that it takes at least 500 years of plate motion to set the stage for such big earthquakes.

The land that was uplifted — and sometimes dropped — by the Great Alaskan Quake also fits with the plate-tectonics model. As convergent plates collide, the overriding — or top — plate gets slowly pushed up. It’s a bit like a rug wrinkling. When the fault finally slips, it’s like a rug flattening out. Also just like a rug, the land spreads out as it flattens.

In Alaska, the flattening caused some of the impacted land to drop. And it moved parts of the Alaskan coast 15 meters (50 feet) toward the sea. Meanwhile, land near the coast just above the fault rose when the fault slipped. This pattern has since been seen at other subduction zones.
But Plafker saw it first, there in Alaska.
Big picture

Geologists call plate tectonics a “unifying theory.” That’s because it explains practically everything about Earth’s crust. It explains how the continents and oceans have been shaped, and why there are earthquakes, mountains, volcanoes and tsunamis.

Naturally, most of the action occurs along the edges of tectonic plate. That is where the plates push into, against or away from each other. Imagine traveling from New Zealand clockwise around the rim of the Pacific Ocean, all the way to the tip of South America. The route is dotted by some serious volcanoes and wracked by violent earthquakes. It should come as no surprise that this perimeter has been nicknamed the “Ring of Fire.”

The Ring of Fire also marks the edge of the Pacific Plate. In Alaska, that plate dives under the North American Plate. This motion causes earthquakes. In fact, West notes, four out of every five U.S. quakes occur in this northernmost state. Pacific Plate motion also creates volcanoes as rock melted by the sinking plate rises to the surface. In Alaska, more than 50 volcanoes have erupted over the last 250 years.
Farther to the south, the Pacific Plate slides past the North American Plate. There, in California, that motion causes earthquakes along the infamous San Andreas Fault.

Scientists are still working out many details of plate tectonics. But Alaska's 1964 Great Quake led to a huge leap in their understanding. "In that one quake, it was the first time that you could actually see plate motion in action. It was the first time we had physical proof of plate tectonics," concludes Mike West.

options.label: The behavior of the Earth during the Great Quake in Alaska helped confirm scientists' theory about the movement of the Earth's plates.
The Great Quake is Alaska showed people how destructive nature could be and helped them develop better safety protocols.
Before the Great Quake in Alaska, scientists did not suspect that the Earth was made up of moving and colliding plates.
While the Great Quake convinced many about the existence of tectonic plates, some people continue to believe that the theory is false.

articles.title: Lessons from failure: Why we try, try again
articles.subtitle: Your mindset after facing failure can make all the difference
articles.author: Bethany Brookshire
articles.description: Bethany Brookshire discusses a study that explores what prompts people to try again after a failure.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Bethany Brookshire discusses a study that explores how people respond to failure and what makes them try again.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what makes people try again after failure.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , 2015. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs or textbooks, visit: https://societyforscience.org/permission-republish

blocks.text: Everyone experiences failures. But not everyone brushes themselves off and tries again. A new study shows that focusing on what can be learned from a failure appears to help people persevere — with a better chance of success the next time.

Jamil Bhanji is neuroscientist at Rutgers University in Brunswick, N.J. There are two main parts to any challenge that may cause someone to fail, he says. First, there are the aspects a person can control. Whether students study for a test, for instance, is under their control. But there also are aspects outside people’s control. Getting sick could make someone too tired to study, even if he might want to.

No matter what causes a letdown, there can be many ways to cope, Bhanji explains. One way is to concentrate on what led to the failure in the first place. If someone fails a test, a problem-focused approach might be to study more or better the next time.

But people who fail can also try focusing on emotions, says Bhanji. The test-taker might feel bad now, but he can convince himself that things will look brighter in the morning. Bhanji describes that as an emotion-focused approach.

Bhanji’s team wanted to find out what strategies people use to forge ahead after failing. To test this, they brought 30 volunteers into a lab and had them play a computer game. The game modeled a classroom and the aim was for players to graduate from the class. Those who succeeded would earn $10.

But getting a player’s character to move across the computer screen and pass the class was no easy task. Along the way, players faced setbacks that could return their characters back to where they had started. For instance, one set of players encountered an “exam.” They had to guess at the right answer to a test, pressing the right key to move forward. If they guessed wrong, they moved back to start. Another group of players faced a non-voluntary “course cancellation.” Their players, too, got sent back to the beginning of the game — but there was nothing they could have done to prevent it.
After each “failure,” players were asked if they would like to try again.

The scientists looked at activity levels in parts of each volunteer’s brain as they played. The researchers used a brain-scanning technique known as functional magnetic resonance imaging , or fMRI. It measures where blood flow is highest and lowest. An area with lots of blood flow suggests that brain region is active. The researchers looked for which brain areas’ blood flow changed when the players decided to try again.

They found that activity was reduced in some parts of the brain when players were tackling challenges. For instance, the ventral striatum (VEN-truhl Stry-AY-tum) sits deep in the skull and is important in motivation — such as whether to try again. Activity here dropped off when players brushed off a failure that had been within their control (such as guessing the wrong key and failing that so-called exam). The lower the activity in this brain region, the more likely a player was to give the game another go. Reduced activity in this area may not be pleasant, since it’s associated with getting something wrong. But it also is associated with learning. As they change their behavior, participants might begin to feel they can do better next time.

But when players were faced with a course cancellation — something they couldn’t control — the activity dropped in a different part of their brains. That part is located right above the eyes and called the ventromedial prefrontal cortex (VEN-troh-MEED-ee-uhl Pree-FRON-tul KOR-tex). This area affects how we judge risk, control our emotions and make decisions. And for uncontrollable setbacks, the lower the activity here, the more likely players were to not give up.

After a setback we can’t control, you realize that this “isn’t due to your own actions [and] you can’t correct that behavior,” Bhanji explains. And this is where successful people put more emphasis on interpreting their emotions in a way that allows them to forge ahead. So when failures are beyond someone’s control, he says, rethinking our emotional responses seems to help.
Persevering under pressure

Many failures — from exams to athletics — occur during times of stress. That prompted Bhanji and his team to repeat their experiment. This time, the scientists stressed out their participants before they played the game. This was a physical and mental stress: participants dipped their hands in ice cold water while a video camera recorded their faces.

After this ice-water bath, the group that faced “exams” still kept trying over and over when they failed. But the group facing course cancellations — conditions they could not control — were now more likely to give up. This could mean that when people are under stress, they are only motivated to forge on if they can learn from their setbacks. If failures are beyond their control, stress may make them less able to control their emotions — and persevere.
Bhanji presented the new data October 19 here at the Society for Neuroscience annual meeting.

This study helps scientists understand what helps people surmount setbacks, says Candace Raio. She’s a psychologist at New York University in New York City. But, she warns, the computer game was short, as was the ice-water bath. It would be interesting to see if stress and the ability to learn from mistakes have a similar impact on sticking with longer-term goals, she says. These might include staying in school until you graduate or finishing some long-term project, such as building a game.

Most obstacles “are not entirely under our control, and not entirely out of our control,” Bhanji observes. If people focus on the parts over which they have some control, "they will be more likely to be persistent,” he suspects — even in times of stress.

options.label: The people who are most successful think of failures as caused by uncontrollable forces rather than their own decisions.
Studies have shown that people are more upset about failures caused by their own actions rather than those outside of their control.
In the face of failure, it's beneficial for people to focus on the things they can control, including how they respond to the failure.
People with stress in their lives are less likely to try again after a failure; therefore, it's important to remain calm and positive in the face of failure.

"This could mean that when people are under stress, they are only motivated to forge on if they can learn from their setbacks." (Paragraph 13)

Bhanji's data show people what response to failure will make them likely to try again.

Do you think failure is an important part of success? Why or why not? What can we learn from our failures?

articles.title: This Tiny Animal Is Apocalypse-Proof
articles.subtitle: Asteroids or exploding stars won't wipe out microscopic critters called tardigrades
articles.author: Maria Temming
articles.description: Maria Temming describes a creature that can survive in unforgiving environments.
articles.intro: Water bears, also known as tardigrades, are eight-legged micro-organisms that can survive in apocalyptic environments. In this informational text, Maria Temming further discusses the conditions water bears can live through and what scientists can learn from them.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the various events water bears can survive.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , August 9, 2017. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs or textbooks, visit: https://societyforscience.org/permission-republish

blocks.text: Water bears might end up being the last animals on Earth.
Also known as tardigrades, these microscopic creatures are tough. A new study concludes that they could survive until — several billion years from now — the sun boils Earth’s oceans away.

That’s good news for anyone hoping Earthlings may have company in the universe. If these critters can live through such extreme conditions on our planet, it suggests life might exist in other seemingly inhospitable places.

Other studies had looked at threats to people from space. They focused on such things as asteroids striking Earth, neighboring stars going supernova or huge explosions called gamma-ray bursts. But for the new study, researchers ignored fragile humans. They wanted to know what it would take to wipe out one of the world’s ultimate survivors.
So they turned to tardigrades.

The microscopic critters live all around the world. And they are hardy. Decades without food or water? No problem. Temperatures plummeting to –272° Celsius (–460° Fahrenheit) or skyrocketing to 150 °C (300 °F)? Bring it on. Even the crushing pressure of deep seas doesn’t bother them. Nor does the vacuum of outer space or exposure to extreme radiation.

Water bears are so sturdy that even a nuclear war probably wouldn’t kill them. Neither would global warming nor any astronomical events that wreak havoc on Earth’s atmosphere. But all of these scenarios could doom humans, notes Avi Loeb. He’s an astrophysicist at Harvard University in Cambridge, Mass.

To exterminate tardigrades, something would have to boil the oceans away. (No more water means no more water bears.) So Loeb and his colleagues calculated just how big an asteroid, how strong a supernova, or how powerful a gamma-ray burst would have to be to heat Earth’s oceans that much.

“They actually ran the numbers on everyone’s favorite natural doomsday weapons,” says Seth Shostak. He’s an astronomer at the SETI Institute in Mountain View, Calif. (One of the SETI Institute’s goals is to search for alien life.)
Loeb’s team found that there are only 19 asteroids in the solar system massive enough to wipe out water bears. None are on a collision course with Earth.

A supernova — the explosion of a massive star after it burns through its fuel — would have to take place within 0.13 light-year of Earth. Yet the closest star big enough to go supernova is nearly 147 light-years away.

Then there are gamma-ray bursts. These explosions are thought to come from especially powerful supernovas or colliding stars. They’re also extremely rare. Over a billion years, there’s only about 1 chance in 3 billion of a gamma-ray burst killing off tardigrades, the researchers calculated.
Loeb and his colleagues published their results July 14 in Scientific Reports .

“Makes me wish I were an extremophile like a tardigrade,” says Edward Guinan. He’s an astrophysicist at Villanova University in Pennsylvania who was not involved in the work. An extremophile is a life form that can survive in harsh environments.
But even tardigrades can’t cheat death forever. In the next seven billion years, the sun will swell into a red giant star. It might engulf Earth, and it will surely sizzle away Earth’s water.

Until then, tardigrades can probably resist any other potential apocalypse. This is exciting to researchers who hope that if there’s life elsewhere in the universe, it lasts long enough for us to find it. Notes Shostak, “Life is tough, once it gets going.”

options.label: There are very few unlikely events that could completely eliminate water bears.
Water bears' ability to survive in space proves there are other life forms that exist beyond Earth.
By studying water bears' ability to survive extreme conditions, humans can learn how to better protect themselves.
While many people believe water bears are indestructible, there are actually several things that can kill them.
